preFix.title: Pyrography on curved surfaces
preFix.subtitle: How to burn cleanly on spoons, bowls, and rounded objects
preFix.excerpt: Flat-surface pyrography technique does not transfer directly to curved objects: the tip angle changes on every stroke and the heat distribution shifts with the curve. This technique tutorial covers holding curved work, adjusting tip angle, burning convex and concave surfaces, and the specific challenges of working on spoon and bowl forms.
preFix.sourceNotes: General pyrography technique from traditional decorative woodcraft traditions; curved surface technique from craft practice with spoon and bowl forms.

content.text: On a flat surface, the angle of the

relative to the wood face stays constant through a stroke. On a curved surface, the angle changes continuously. A tip that starts tangent to the surface at the beginning of a stroke will be pointing into the wood or lifting away from it by the end. Learning to adjust the handpiece angle through the stroke is the core skill for curved
. The nichrome wire burner is easier on curves than a solid-tip unit because the fine wire tip is more sensitive to angle changes.

Small curved objects such as spoons need a holding method that does not interfere with the burning hand. A non-slip mat or a sandbag saddle allows the piece to be repositioned quickly without a clamp. For small spoons, the non-burning hand holds the spoon on the mat from below; the burning hand rests its little finger against the back of the spoon as a steadying anchor. Never hold the piece in the air: hand tremor is magnified at the tip end of a heated handpiece.

On the back of a spoon bowl or the outside of a rounded object, the surface curves away from the handpiece at each end of the stroke. Begin with the tip slightly lower than vertical to compensate for the curve at the start; lift slightly through the stroke; and lower again at the end. Keep strokes short: 20 to 30 mm maximum on a tight curve. Longer strokes require too much angle adjustment and produce variable burns.

The inside of a bowl or spoon bowl curves toward the handpiece at each end of the stroke. The tip angle adjustment is the reverse of convex: start slightly high, lower through the middle of the stroke, and lift at the end. Concave surfaces also gather heat slightly more than convex ones; reduce the heat setting by one notch when moving from convex to concave work on the same piece.

heats and cools in under a second rather than the thirty seconds a solid tip needs. This allows the temperature to be adjusted almost continuously for different curve radii on the same piece. The fine wire tip also has a smaller contact area, which means the angle error at the start and end of a stroke has less effect on burn darkness.
Using the same heat setting for flat and curved sections of the same piece: the curve concentrates heat slightly differently. Test the setting on the curve before committing to the design.

Making long strokes across tight curves: each long stroke on a tight curve requires too much handpiece tilt adjustment and produces uneven burns. Work in short 20 mm strokes on any curve tighter than a 50 mm radius.
Holding the piece in the air: even a steady hand transmits tremor at the tip. The piece must rest on a surface or be braced against a stable support.

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
brand-trademark: brand name "Anchor" — generic equivalent is "salted butter"
